coredata.dc:title: N doped-carbon quantum dots with ultra-high quantum yield photoluminescent property conjugated with folic acid for targeted drug delivery and bioimaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Khoshnood, Ali
Farhadian, Nafiseh
Abnous, Khalil
Matin, Maryam M.
Ziaee, Nasrin
Yaghoobi, Elnaz
coredata.dc:description:
Background Improving targeted therapy, and reducing adverse side effects of antitumor drugs toward normal cells with simultaneous bioimaging properties are being developed in pharmaceutical industries. In this study, N doped-carbon quantum dot (CQD) with ultra-high quantum yield (QY) was synthesized and its surface was modified with folic acid as the targeted anticancer agent.
Methods CQD was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The impact of operating parameters such as pyrolysis temperature, time, and pH on the QY and surface charge was investigated. Then, the CQD surface was modified with folic acid as the targeted anticancer agent followed by doxorubicin (DOX) conjugation on the surface. Cytotoxicity of CQD-FA and CQD-FA-DOX upon 4T1, MCF7 and PC12 cell lines were examined. Furthermore, fluorescent microscopy imaging and flow cytometry analysis for various cell lines were applied to evaluate CQD-FA bioimaging property. In addition, the efficacy of the formulation on the BALB/c mice bearing breast cancer tumors was investigated.
Results In CQD preparation, average particle size of 7 nm was confirmed by the characterization results. With optimum synthesis conditions of pH = 11, time = 8 h and temperature = 160 °C, QY was about 90%. XPS analysis confirmed formation of N doped CQD. DOX loading efficiency was 88.6% and about 86% of the loaded DOX was released from CQD-FA-DOX after 72 h at pH = 5.5. Cytotoxicity analysis confirmed a higher toxicity effect of CQD-FA-DOX toward cancerous cell lines (4T1, MCF7) in comparison to free DOX. This nanocarrier could act smartly in identifying folate receptor positive cancerous cells and enter mainly by the receptor-mediated route. In vivo results showed that cellular internalization of CQD-FA-DOX was greater than free DOX, in which the tumor growth in mice treated with CQD-FA-DOX was lower than free DOX. In addition, ex vivo analysis confirmed lower side effects of CQD-FA-DOX on the main organs of mice in comparison to the free drug group.
Conclusion This new synthesized carrier can be a suitable platform for drug delivery applications with lower side effects, targeted cellular uptake and bioimaging properties.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving targeted therapy, and reducing adverse side effects of antitumor drugs toward normal cells with simultaneous bioimaging properties are being developed in pharmaceutical industries. In this study, N doped-carbon quantum dot (CQD) with ultra-high quantum yield (QY) was synthesized and its surface was modified with folic acid as the targeted anticancer agent.

CQD was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The impact of operating parameters such as pyrolysis temperature, time, and pH on the QY and surface charge was investigated. Then, the CQD surface was modified with folic acid as the targeted anticancer agent followed by doxorubicin (DOX) conjugation on the surface. Cytotoxicity of CQD-FA and CQD-FA-DOX upon 4T1, MCF7 and PC12 cell lines were examined. Furthermore, fluorescent microscopy imaging and flow cytometry analysis for various cell lines were applied to evaluate CQD-FA bioimaging property. In addition, the efficacy of the formulation on the BALB/c mice bearing breast cancer tumors was investigated.

In CQD preparation, average particle size of 7 nm was confirmed by the characterization results. With optimum synthesis conditions of pH = 11, time = 8 h and temperature = 160 °C, QY was about 90%. XPS analysis confirmed formation of N doped CQD. DOX loading efficiency was 88.6% and about 86% of the loaded DOX was released from CQD-FA-DOX after 72 h at pH = 5.5. Cytotoxicity analysis confirmed a higher toxicity effect of CQD-FA-DOX toward cancerous cell lines (4T1, MCF7) in comparison to free DOX. This nanocarrier could act smartly in identifying folate receptor positive cancerous cells and enter mainly by the receptor-mediated route. In vivo results showed that cellular internalization of CQD-FA-DOX was greater than free DOX, in which the tumor growth in mice treated with CQD-FA-DOX was lower than free DOX. In addition, ex vivo analysis confirmed lower side effects of CQD-FA-DOX on the main organs of mice in comparison to the free drug group.
This new synthesized carrier can be a suitable platform for drug delivery applications with lower side effects, targeted cellular uptake and bioimaging properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the most chronic life-threatening diseases for humans globally. Breast cancer is the second-leading cause of cancer related death worldwide and one of the most common malignancies among women. Although, impressive methods have been advanced for breast cancer diagnosis and treatment over the last decades, it is still one of the leading causes of death. There are different treatments for breast cancer. Surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy are the common standard treatments. Chemotherapy is a common way to treat cancer, which aims to destroy cancer cells. Today, the use of chemotherapy in the treatment of cancer has increased to over 50 types of drugs. These drugs can target proteins, RNAs and DNAs [1]. Doxorubicin (DOX) is one of the most extensively used anticancer drugs since the 1960 s [2–4].

Doxorubicin is an antibiotic obtained from the bacterium Streptomyces peucetius. Doxorubicin is part of the anthracycline chemotherapy drug group. It is common to use doxorubicin to treat soft tissue and bone sarcomas, various types of cancers, including breast, ovarian, bladder, and thyroid cancers [5].Doxorubicin is a strong iron chelator. The mechanism of its effect is through the connections made between DNA base pairs and preventing the formation of ester bonds, inhibiting the topoisomerase II enzyme and opening the double-stranded helix of DNA. It causes DNA disintegration and inhibition of DNA and RNA synthesis. Then, through the binding of the iron-doxorubicin complex to DNA and cell membrane and the production of free radicals, it causes the cell membrane and DNA to disintegrate [6]. The majority of existing anticancer drugs suffer from low selectivity and distinction between normal and cancer cells, which leads to systemic toxicity. This significantly limits the maximum permissible dose of the drug. Applying drug delivery systems is widely recommended to reduce the dosage quantity of drugs and side effects with distinctive functions on the target tumors. Additionally, it may improve the delivery of poorly water-soluble drugs, overcoming multidrug resistance [7–9].

Cancerous cells are capable to run away from the surveillance mechanisms of the immune system and form a unique permissive setting, the so-called tumor microenvironment (TME). Furthermore, they need to feed the nutrients necessary to fuel their continued growth [10,11]. Thus, overexpression of specific receptors and tumor-associated surface antigens plays a unique role in using antibodies or corresponding ligand-mediated drug delivery systems for the identification and specific tumor targeting [9].

Folic acid (FA) has been regarded as a primary ligand for specific labeling of some cancer cells like malignancies of the ovary, mammary gland, lung, kidney, breast, brain, prostate, and throat, owning to overly expressed folate receptor (FR) on the membrane of these cancerous cells. As folate receptors are transmembrane proteins, they play a critical role in the transport of FA into cells. FA bind to these receptors with high binding affinity which makes them a target for FA-based therapies. Compared to other targeting molecules, FA has many benefits, including low molecular weight, stability, high susceptibility to organic and aqueous solvents and low immunogenicity [7,9,12]. Nowadays, conjugating FA with some fluorescent nanoparticles has been widely employed for tumor targeted imaging and diagnosis.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD), a novel rising star of fluorescent nanoparticles, have attracted many researchers owing to their exceptional properties such as high chemical and optical stability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, cost-effective synthesis and tunable optical properties [12–14]. Their rich surface of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups confers facile surface modification for biomedicine applications such as drug delivery and cell imaging all-in-one platform [13,15,16]. Considering the importance of quantum yield (QY) and carbon fluorescence emission of quantum dots in the field of cell imaging and drug delivery, it is of great importance to design and optimize the synthesis process to achieve CQD with high QY.

The main novelty of this study is synthesizing CQD with ultra-high quantum yield photoluminescent property for simultaneous drug delivery and bioimaging applications. Hydrothermal process was applied for CQD synthesis. Optimizing the operating conditions such as time, pH and pyrolysis temperature for synthesis of CQD with high QY was examined. Then, a novel, simple, cheap and efficient purification method was used to achieve CQD with ultra-high quantum yield. This is another novelty of this study. After that, the best operating conditions to achieve CQD with high QY were examined. In addition, the surface of CQD was conjugated with FA by formation a covalent bond between amino groups of CQD and carboxylic groups of FA. Then, DOX drug was loaded on this new carrier. Cytotoxicity of the new carrier against various cancerous cell lines as well as its ability for bioimaging, was analyzed in detail. After that, the efficacy of the nanocarrier for the treatment of BALB/c mice bearing breast cancer tumors was investigated.

CQD synthesis: Citric acid (CA), Polyethylene glycol with a molecular weight of 2000 Da (PEG-2000), Ethylenediamine (EDA), Folic acid (FA), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), Neutral red (NR), and Aluminum oxide (Al2O3) were purchased from Merck company (Germany). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was purchased from Euroasia Co., Ltd. (India).

Cell culture: Human breast cancer cells (MCF7), mouse breast cancer cells (4T1), and rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cells (PC12) were obtained from the Pasteur Institute of Iran. Cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (Gibco) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (Sigma-Aldrich).

Animals: Female BALB/c mice (4–6 weeks of age, 18–20 g) were purchased from the Razi Vaccine and Serum Research Institute, North East Branch, Mashhad, Iran. The mice were kept in average humidity of 60 ± 5% and controlled temperature of 25 ± 2˚C at specified-pathogen-free conditions. With the aim of taking measures to protect the animals from pain and discomfort, experiments were carried out according to the ethical protocol approved by the Mashhad University of Medical Science.

CQD was synthesized through a hydrothermal method using CA, EDA, and PEG-2000 as a precursor. Briefly, 2.5 g CA and 0.5 g PEG-2000 were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water and stirred for 5 min to form a clear solution. Then, 5 ml EDA was added into the solution and transferred to a 50 ml high-pressure reactor (autoclave) for 8 h reaction time at 160 °C. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally and a clear light yellow–brown solution was obtained [17].

In order to determine the impact of pyrolysis temperature, time, and pH on zeta potential and QY of synthesized CQD, several experiments were designed and carried out. In the first series of experiments, at the constant reaction time = 8 h and pH = 11, experiments were carried out at selected temperatures of 160, 180 and 200 °C. In the second series of tests, the optimal temperature was selected from the previous tests and the experiments were performed at pH = 11 and time = 6, 8, and 10 h. In the third series of experiments, syntheses were performed at pH = 11, 9, 7, and 5 at optimal temperature and times were taken from the previous experiments. All experiments were repeated 3 times and results were statistically compared to each other in each series using GradPad Prism software, version 9.5.1.

In order to purify CQD and achieve the maximum quantum yield, the excess reagents must be removed from the solution. Purification of CQD is not a simple and common work. In most cases, CQD disperse in water excellent and are small enough to be separated by filtration or centrifugation. In some cases, dialysis membrane was applied for CQD separation [18]. However, this procedure is time consuming, difficult to be scaled up, relatively expensive and normally doesn’t separate possible large nanostructured by-products such as polymers. Some studies applied HPLC or silica column chromatography to purify and separate carbon dotes [19,20]. Although, these techniques are efficient, but they are expensive. Here, a novel, simple, economical, and effective procedure for the purification of CQD is presented based on the solid phase extraction, using alumina as solid phase. This method has very low cost and affords highly pure CQD in short time, compared to the time-consuming purification with dialysis membranes or high cost HPLC technique. In this method, passing CQD solution through an aluminum oxide solid phase bed, centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then purification with dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) for 12 h against 1 L of deionized water was performed. By-products of CQD are organic compounds, often having heterocyclic rings that formed from condensation of citric acid with diamines [21]. These compounds trapped in alumina bed and CQD were extracted from alumina bed using water. The final solution was dried for future characterization analysis with a freeze dryer (24 h, −40 °C).

FTIR analysis (Thermo nicolet 370, USA) was used to identify functional groups of samples. Ultra-violet absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained by UV-1800 (Shimadzu, Japan) and RF-540 (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy)EDS(analysis (BRNO-MIRA3 LMU, TESCAN) was used to identify the elements and their percentages in the sample. Element analysis and surface composition were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Bes Tec, Germany) by magnesium anode source (Mg Ka, hu = 1253.6 eV). Surface charge and hydrodynamic diameter of samples were investigated by Zetasizer (Malvern Instrument Ltd., Worcestershire, UK) based on the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique at the temperature of 25 °C. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to determine the morphology and structure of samples taken by FEI TECNAI F20. Cellular uptake capability was determined using flow cytometry (BD Accuri C6, USA) analysis.

The QY of CQD was calculated using the Eq. (1):
(1)
∅ =

where Φ is QY, the subscripts “st” and “C” refer to the standard and CQD samples, respectively. K is the measured slope by the curves of integral fluorescence and absorbance. Moreover, η is the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate solution (dissolved at 0.1 M H2SO4) with a known QY was used as fluorescence standard (QY = 54%, η = 1.33) [22].

To calculate the quantum yield, the dried CQD obtained from the synthesis step were diluted in deionized water to make six concentrations <1 ppm. Then, fluorescence spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm for each concentration. A graph in terms of surface integrals below the fluorescence spectrum and the intensity of UV absorption at 360 nm for the same concentrations were plotted. By calculating the slope of the line passing through the points, Kc was determined. To calculate Kst , the previous steps for the standard sample were repeated.

FA was conjugated to the CQD surface via the covalent bond between carboxyl groups of FA and amino groups of CQD. Three different methods were applied for FA conjugation on the CQD surface (see supporting file). Achievement the greater fluorescence emission was our goal in selection the best method. Here, the details of the best method are described in details. First, 2 mg EDC, 3 mg NHS and 4.6 mg FA were dissolved in 20 ml Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer (pH = 7.4) and stirred overnight at ambient temperature in dark. Then, 3 ml of CQD (0.01 g.ml −1) was added to the FA mixture and stirred at the same condition for 24 h. Finally, the obtained CQD-FA was centrifuged and dialyzed (MWCO = 3500 Da) against 200 ml deionized water for 4 h by changing water every 2 h [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and drug release behavior
ce:section.ce:para:
For drug loading on the CQD-FA, 1 ml CQD-FA (200 µg. ml−1) and 1 ml DOX (400 µg. ml−1) were mixed with 2 ml PBS (pH = 7.4). For complete attachment, the solution was then allowed to shake overnight at 200 rpm. Then, it was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) with 40 ml deionized water to remove any unloaded DOX. The amount of DOX outside the dialysis bag was calculated by the DOX photoluminescence (PL) intensity calibration curve (excited at λ = 485 nm [24]). The drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug loading content (DLC) were calculated using the following equations [25]:
(2)
D L E
%
=
(

DOX

)

× 100
(3)

)

mDOX represents the initial mass of DOX and mfree refers to the mass of unloaded DOX in the water surrounding the dialysis bag, and mNP represents the initial mass of the CQD-FA. In order to determine the optimal concentration of DOX for loading on the CQD, different DOX concentrations of 100, 200, 400, 600, and 800 µg. ml−1 were examined. For each concentration, DLE and DLC were calculated and results were statistically compared to each other using GradPad Prism software, version 9.5.1.

To measure the drug release, 1 ml CQD-FA-DOX solution (containing 44 µg.ml−1 of DOX) was sealed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) and dialyzed against 10 ml PBS (pH = 7.4) and acetate buffer (pH = 5.5) separately and incubated in a shaker incubator (37 °C, 80 rpm). At selected time intervals, 500 µl of buffer solution outside the dialysis bag was taken out to be analyzed by fluorescence spectrophotometry and replaced with 500 µl of fresh buffer.
Finally, the mass of the drug removed from the system due to sampling was added to the mass of the drug released at the next time and cumulative drug release was plotted versus time.

The neutral red (NR) method was utilized to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CQD-FA and CQD-FA-DOX. MCF7 and 4T1 cell lines were selected as folate receptor (FR+) and PC12 was applied since it doesn’t have folate receptor (FR-). In this regard, MCF7, 4T1 and PC12 cell lines were seeded at the density of 5000 cells per well in 96-well plates and kept for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C under 5% CO2. After that, the culture media were replaced with fresh media containing different amounts of CQD-FA, CQD-FA-DOX and free DOX. In the case of CQD-FA, they were incubated for another 24 h. In terms of CQD-FA-DOX and free DOX, after 3 h, the culture medium was replaced with fresh medium without drugs and incubated for 48 h.

The neutral red method was used to calculate cell viability as a percentage. For this purpose, 4 mg neutral red powder was dissolved in 1 ml PBS (pH = 7.4), then 120 µl of the solution was added to 12 ml culture medium and incubated overnight at 37 °C. Afterward, the neutral red medium was centrifuged for 10 min at 2000 rpm to remove any precipitated dye. For each well of the plate, the cell culture medium was replaced with 100 µl neutral red medium and incubated for 3 h. Then, the neutral red medium was removed, and cells were washed with 150 µl PBS (pH = 7.4). 150 µl destain solution (10 ml deionized water + 10 ml ethanol 96% + 0.2 ml glacial acetic acid) was added to wells and the plates were shaken rapidly for 10 min. Finally, the optical density (OD) was determined at 540 nm [26].

To evaluate the potential capability of CQD-FA for cell imaging, MCF7, 4T1, and PC12 cells were seeded in 24-well plates at the density of 3000 cells/well and kept for 24 h in an incubator. Then, they were treated with a fresh medium containing CQD-FA (250 µg/ml), CQD-FA-DOX or free DOX (same DOX concentration, 3.2 µM), respectively for 3 h. After that, the cells were washed with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. The fluorescent images were acquired with a fluorescent microscope (λex: 460–480 nm).

Flowcytometry analysis is a useful analysis to investigate binding of the drug to the cell membranes or its penetration into the cells. This analysis enables researchers to investigate cellular uptake for various drug formulations. Some studies applied this method in drug delivery field [27,28]. Here, the cellular internalization ability of CQD-FA-DOX and free DOX was achieved using the BD FACS Calibur flow cytometer with a 488 nm laser in the FL2 channel. Cancer cell lines MCF7, 4T1 and PC12 (150 × 103 cells/well) were cultured in 12-well plates. After 24 h, CQD-FA-DOX, free DOX (DOX equivalent concentration 2 µg/ml) and PBS were added to wells and incubated for 3 h. The treated cells were washed with PBS, trypsinized, and centrifuged for 5 min at 1500 rpm after removing their media. The cell pellet was resuspended in 400 μl cold PBS for flow cytometry analysis [25]. All data were analyzed with FlowJo 10.8.1.

To investigate the in vivo function of the samples, 25 BALB/c inbred female mice (aged 4–6 weeks, and weighing 18–20 g) used. BALB/c mice were transplanted with 5 × 105 4T1 cells suspended in PBS (pH = 7.4) by injection into the right flank of each mouse. The mice were divided into five groups at random (5 mice in each group) as:
Group (1): PBS (negative control).
Group (2): Free DOX (positive control).
Group (3): CQD-FA.
Group (4): CQD-DOX.
Group 5: CQD-FA-DOX.

When the tumor size reached 40–50 mm3, 200 µl of each sample, according to the group number, was injected through the tail vein. The control group was treated with 200 µl PBS (pH = 7.4). It should be noted that the content of DOX in each group was based on 0.6 mg DOX/kg of mice body weight. The size of the tumors was monitored every 3 days for 21 days. In order to calculate tumor volume, the following formula was used:
(4)
Tumor
volume = w i d t h × l e n g t h × h e i g h t × 0.5

To investigate the systemic toxicity of the prepared formulations, 21 days after treatment, the tissues (heart, liver, spleen and tumor) from each group of mice were harvested and fixed in 10% formalin solution. Then, the tissue sections with 5 µm thickness were mounted onto the glass slides. After staining with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), the samples were photographed using an optical microscope.

Four BALB/c mice with about 300 mm3 4T1 tumor size were intravenously injected through the tail veil with CQD-FA-DOX and free DOX (DOX equivalent concentration was 0.6 mg/kg). At certain time points (6 and 24 h), the mice were anesthetized, and the heart, liver, kidney, spleen and tumor were isolated. The images were captured using the animal body imaging device based on the fluorescence background of each organ at λex = 450 nm and λem = 790 nm.

To confirm the successful synthesis of CQD, the UV spectra of the final sample were measured. As shown in Fig. 1 a, a sharp UV absorption peak at λ = 240 nm could be mainly attributed to π- π* transitions of the aromatic ring (C = C) related to the core of CQD and a broad peak approximately at λ = 350 nm referred to n-π* transitions of C = O bonds corresponding to the functional groups at the surface of CQD [29–31].

The chemical structure of CQD was characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the broad peak at λ = 3406 cm−1 is attributed to –NH2 and –OH groups (stretching) [17]. The peak at λ = 2880 cm−1 originated from C-H (stretching) [32]. The C = O (stretching) was confirmed by the peak at λ = 1647 cm−1 [33]. The peaks observed at λ = 1564 cm−1 correspond to N-H (bending). The peaks located at λ = 1344 cm−1, 1284 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 could be assigned to –OH (bending), –CH (bending) and C-O (stretching), respectively [34,35].

HRTEM images (Fig. 1c) illustrate spherical CQD with uniform particle size. The histogram of particle size distribution between 7 and 10 nm and average particle size distribution of 8–9 nm are reported in the diagram of Fig. 3c. In addition, it confirms the lattice spacing with d = 0.22 nm that corresponds to the in-plane lattice spacing, which indicates graphite phase structure [36,37].

XPS measurements can be used to identify the element, its chemical state, and its quantity. As Fig. 2
a shows, the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms is identified in the sample. Atomic percentages based on the area peaks under the XPS spectrum were 48.71, 3.69 and 47.60 for C, N and O atoms, respectively. In the high-resolution of C 1 s XPS spectrum of the CQD (Fig. 2b), the peaks with the binding energy of 285 eV, 286.4 eV, and 287.5 eV can be ascribed to C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N, and C = O bonds, respectively [38–40]. The core-level spectrum of N 1 s for the CQD displayed in Fig. 2c, was deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 399.2 eV and 400.2 eV, which represent pyrrolic-N and N–H, respectively [41,42]. This approves formation of N doped-CQD. Moreover, the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) is composed of three peaks at 532.2 eV, 532.8 eV, and 533.5 eV assigned to the form of C = O, –COOH, -C–OH bonds, respectively [38–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the operating parameters of CQD synthesis

ce:section.ce:para: Enhancing the pH value of the solution from 5 up to 11 increases the QY from 43.76 to 88.32 (Fig. 3a). The surface charge of synthesized CQD changes from −12.38 to −24.46. According to the EDS analysis spectrum (Table 1 ), the number of nitrogen atoms in the sample with pH = 11, 9, 7 decreased to 23.24%, 16.53% and 9.99%, respectively. Lowering the pH, related to the reaction between amine groups of EDA and acids, ultimately leads to the production of ammonium salts, and the amination has not been done well. However, pH = 11 was selected as the optimum value.

Fig. 3b shows the effect of pyrolysis temperature on the QY and surface charge of CQD. As the figure shows, the maximum QY has been observed at the temperature of 160 °C (QY = 88.32%). Previous studies showed that CQD with high quantum efficiency are formed when the reaction temperature is close to the melting temperature of the primary precursor [44,45]. The melting temperature of citric acid as the primary precursor is 153–160 °C; the maximum quantum yield was obtained close to the melting temperature at 160 °C. Since carbon dots emission often originates from the surface state, the high quantum yield at 160 °C indicates the synergistic effect of the core and surface. Thus, at 160 °C, the process of carbonization containing the formation of the core and the surface of CQD was well done. By increasing the reaction temperature from 160 °C to 200 °C, overproduction of carbon particles and other by-products occurs and the effect of surface state on the quantum yield became weak and QY was decreased [46–49].

Furthermore, to determine the elemental analysis of each CQD, EDS analysis was performed for temperatures of 160, 180 and 200 °C. As Table 1 shows the content of carbon increases from 43.42% at the temperature of 160 °C to 57.59% at the temperature of 180 °C. While nitrogen content has decreased from 23.24% to 4.88%. This confirms the excessive carbonization process at high temperatures and consequently a reduction in the formation of amine functional groups on the CQD surfaces.

As Fig. 3c shows, increasing the reaction time from 6 h to 8 h increased the QY from 79.6% to 88.32%, which indicates the optimal reaction temperature for the carbonization process. Further increase of the reaction time from 8 h to 10 h can lead to the overproduction of carbon particles. In this case, while the effect of the nucleus on the quantum efficiency was stronger, its surface state effect was reduced, and finally, the quantum yield was reduced [46–49].

EDS analysis for the reaction time of 8 h and 10 h shows that carbon content increases from 43.42% at 8 h to 52.31% at 10 h, which confirms the excessive production of carbon. The nitrogen content decrease from 23.24% at 8 h (zeta potential: −24:46) to 9.6% at 10 h (zeta potential: −27.26) may indicate the decrease of amino groups on the surface of CQD.
Finally, the optimum operating parameters were selected as pH = 11, T = 160 °C and reaction time of 8 h.

To confirm the successful conjugation of CQD with FA, UV visible spectrum of CQD-FA was measured and compared with pure CQD and FA spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the characteristic absorption peaks of CQD-FA at λ = 283 nm and λ = 353 nm indicate the successful immobilization of FA onto the surface of CQD. Furthermore, zeta potential measurements proved the successful conjugation of FA to CQD. The negative zeta potential value of CQD was changed from −24.46 mV to −30.28 mV for CQD-FA. This may due to covering the amino groups of CQD by FA or presence of FA with negative carboxylic groups on the surface of CQD [50]. It should be noted that the amino groups of CQD bond covalently with the carboxylic groups of FA, through EDC and NHS as a classical cross-linking reaction [33].

Results of DLE and DLC using Eqs. (2) and (3) for various DOX concentration is shown in Fig. 4b, 4c. Results revealed that in DLE graph, there is a significant difference between DOX concentrations of 600 and 800 µg. ml−1. However, in DLC graph, a significant difference between the concentrations of 200 and 400 µg.ml−1 was observed. Finally, DOX concentration of 400 µg.ml−1 was selected as the optimum value. At this DOX concentration, calculated DLE and DLC were 88.6% and 177.2 mg. g−1, respectively. Moreover, changing the zeta potential value from −30.28 mV for CQD-FA to −7.5 mV for CQD-FA-DOX approves DOX loading with a positive charge of amine groups. There are three possibilities for loading DOX on CQD [24,34,51]:
i)
Hydrogen bond: Excellent hydrophilicity of CQD with –COOH and –NH2 functional groups can form hydrogen bonds with functional groups of DOX.
ii)
Electrostatic interactions: There is also the possibility of electrostatic interactions due to the negative charge of CQD and the positive charge of DOX. The strongest pka of doxorubicin is equal to 9.46. At the pH of drug loading, DOX has a positive surface charge.
iii)
π-π interactions: Presence of the aromatic rings in CQD and DOX can make π-π interactions between nanocarrier and drug.

Results of drug release during 72 h at two different pH = 5.5 (environment of cancer cells) and pH = 7.4 (healthy physiological environment) are shown in Fig. 4d. As shown in the figure, after 72 h, the rate of drug release at pH = 5.5 (86.4%) was higher than that at pH = 7.4 (44.6%). This may be related to the acidic conditions of the environment at pH = 5.5 that dissociate hydrogen bonding, electrostatic or π-π interactions of DOX with CQD-FA surface. On the other hand, the –NH2 groups in DOX are converted to protons in acidic conditions, which increases the hydrophilicity of DOX. Subsequently, the solubility of DOX was increased, and it separated from the surface of the CQD-FA [24,34,51]. Due to different DOX release patterns from CQD-FA under various pHs, this multi-functionalized CQD-FA can be used as a qualified candidate for tumor chemotherapy [52,53].

Prism software was used to analyze the cytotoxicity data. Two-way ANOVA was employed to analyze the difference between study groups. P value of 0.05 was deemed significant. ****, ***, ** and * was referred to p-value < 0.0001, p-value < 0.001, p-value < 0.01 and p-value < 0.05. The number of repeats was n = 6.

The biocompatibility of CQD plays an essential role in diagnosis and treatment of cancer cells. Thus, the cytotoxicity of the CQD-FA was investigated using Neutral red assay to make sure it has low toxicity. The results of the CQD-FA toxicity analysis on three cell lines of MCF7, 4T1 (FR+ as target cells), and PC12 (FR-) are shown in Fig. 5 a. It is obvious that even at the high concentration of CQD-FA (1 mg.ml −1), more than 70% of the cells remained alive after 24 h of incubation. However, acute cytotoxicity was not observed for CQD-FA.

According to the results of Neutral red assay for CQD-FA-DOX complex on three cell lines 4T1, MCF7 and PC12 and comparing them with free DOX, it was found that CQD-FA-DOX was more effective on FR+ cells in comparison to free DOX (Fig. 5a). It confirms successful targeting of cancerous cells containing folate receptors with CQD-FA-DOX. This targeted delivery leads to higher concentrations of DOX within the cancer cells compared to free DOX, resulting in increased cell death in 4T1 and MCF7 cells. Conversely, PC12 cells without folate receptors are not targeted by CQD-FA-DOX and had lower cell death in comparison to free Dox (Fig. 5b-5d). This approves an excellent anticancer selectivity of CQD-FA-DOX.

The cellular uptake behavior of CQD-FA-DOX was evaluated using fluorescent microscopy for 4T1 (FR+), MCF7 (FR+), and PC12 (FR-) as control cells (Fig. 6 ). The bright photoluminescence of 4T1 and MCF7 cells confirmed that CQD-FA-DOX uptake by the cells owing to the high-affinity binding of FA to FR, while no fluorescence was observed for PC12 cells. This indicates that CQD-FA-DOX could localize in the cytoplasm via receptor-mediated endocytosis [9,54].

Flow cytometry analysis was also performed to investigate the cellular uptake ability of CQD-FA-DOX and free dox in 4T1, MCF7 and PC12 cells. As shown in Fig. 7 , DOX is known to be transported into the cells via passive diffusion [28], leading to high fluorescence intensity recorded in the diagrams of all three cell lines. In contrast, CQD-FA-DOX (red histogram) showed stronger fluorescence emission than free DOX (orange histogram) in 4T1 and MCF7 cell lines, indicating that the CQD-FA-DOX is able to deliver a higher concentration of DOX to the target cells through its ability to identify folate receptors on the surface of breast cancer cells, which primarily enter cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. However, the cellular uptake of free DOX was greater than that of CQD-FA-DOX in PC12 cells as FR-.

To explore the biodistribution of injected CQD-DOX, CQD-FA-DOX and free DOX; the ex vivo fluorescence images of isolated organs, including spleen, liver, lung, heart, kidney and tumor (n = 1) were investigated at 6 and 24 h post-injection (Figs. 8, 9
). Results showed the maximum fluorescence signal was observed in the tumor tissue of CQD-FA-DOX mouse compared to other tissues. It demonstrates that the CQD-FA-DOX has more accumulation in the tumor site with binding to the folate receptors of the cancerous cells. This approves the selectivity and targeting of the synthesized complex toward tumors with FR due to the high affinity of folate-conjugated nanoparticles for cancer cells, leading to increased accumulation of the CQD-FA-DOX in the tumor tissue.

The in vivo therapeutic efficiency of the system was evaluated by monitoring the tumor volume every 3 days after treatment with PBS, free DOX, CQD-FA, CQD-DOX and CQD-FA-DOX. The results obtained from Fig. 10 shows that treatment with PBS had no therapeutic effect as the tumor volume increased rapidly from about 70 mm3 to approximately 3270 mm3 over a period of 21 days. In comparison, all DOX groups exhibited inhibition of tumor growth rate. Notably, CQD-DOX showed a more controlled growth rate than free DOX, which indicated that the Nano-drug delivery system can leads to increased drug accumulation at the tumor site. Moreover, the FA targeting ability of CQD-FA-DOX contributed to a lower growth rate in the tumor compared to other treatments. This was associated with a greater concentration of DOX delivered specifically to the targeted tumor site, ultimately resulting in a significant improvement in the antitumor effect.

As shown in Fig. 11 , H&E staining of tissues (spleen, liver, and heart) demonstrated that no prominent organ was damaged in the CQD-FA, CQD-DOX and CQD-FA-DOX groups. However, mild necrotic degeneration of cardiomyocytes (red arrows) associated with mild inflammatory cell infiltration (black arrow) was observed in the heart tissue of the free DOX group. Furthermore, tumor cells with disorder and different sizes associated with mitotic figures (black arrow) were detected in the PBS, CQD-FA and CQD-DOX treated groups. Also, tumor cells with disorders and different sizes associated with mitotic figures (black arrow) and necrosis (N) were noticed in free DOX and CQD-FA-DOX treated groups.

Here, an ultra-high QY and photoluminescent CQD was synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. By applying a novel purification method and optimizing the synthesis conditions, N doped-CQD with high QY ∼ 90% were synthesized. Then, CQD was conjugated with FA for targeting folate receptor positive cancerous cells, and then DOX was loaded on this complex. Cellular cytotoxicity, ex vivo, and in vivo performance of the nanocarrier were examined. Results confirmed the successful formation of CQD-FA-DOX with high efficacy on FR+ 4T1 and MCF7 cancerous cells. In vivo performance of the complex on the mice bearing breast cancer cells confirmed significant control of tumor growth in the group treated with CQD-FA-DOX compared to the commercial DOX ampoule. Furthermore, no serious toxic effects were observed in the CQD-FA-DOX-treated mice based on the histopathological analysis. Finally, CQD-FA-DOX can be a suitable chemotherapy agent with dual high QY and bioimaging properties.
